You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

persona: Sumit Sutradhar is a 30‑year‑old packer who blends hands‑on skill with a curiosity for small innovations, they juggle village traditions, modest savings, and a lingering back ache while dreaming of supervising a team.
cultural_background: Sumit belongs to a Bengali rural community in South 24 Parganas, West Bengal. His cultural life is shaped by agrarian rhythms, seasonal festivals such as Durga Puja, Raja, and local village rituals honoring deities like Shitala. He grew up in a joint family where respect for elders, communal cooperation, and simple folk customs are valued. While his upbringing emphasizes tradition and practicality, his innate curiosity leads him to appreciate modest innovations—like observing new packing methods in nearby markets—and to blend them with established ways.

He also understands spoken English well enough to follow simple instructions at work and to interact with supervisors, though he cannot read or write in either language due to his illiteracy. His functional literacy is limited to recognizing common symbols, numerals, and basic signboards, which he relies on for tasks like counting packages or reading labels.

He is proficient in basic inventory tracking using visual cues, and can quickly count and organize up to dozens of packages. His physical stamina enables him to work long shifts under varying weather conditions. He possesses basic arithmetic skills for tallying items, time management to meet shipping deadlines, and the ability to coordinate with fellow workers to ensure smooth workflow. Additionally, his curiosity drives him to observe and adopt small improvements in packing techniques.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

GENERATION STAGE = ENGLISH PIVOT: write clinical prose in English (Latin script). A later pipeline stage translates prose into the target Indic language/script. Do NOT write the document body in Bengali/Hindi/Tamil/etc. at this stage.

CRITICAL — TYPE names are NEVER translated: always exact ASCII uppercase allow-list ids, e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]] NOT [[রোগীর_নাম|…]] or [[रोगी_नाम|…]] or [[PatientName|…]].

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- At generation (English pivot): write clinical prose in English. TYPE names stay exact ASCII allow-list forever (PATIENT_NAME, MRN, …). NEVER translate or localize the TYPE token itself. A later stage translates prose to the target Indic script.

- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.

- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.

- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.

- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- Aadhaar must be 12 digits, leading 2–9, Verhoeff-valid (synthetic checksum OK).
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names — copy spelling exactly):

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

BAD → missing tags / HOSPITAL_ID only:
  Facility name in prose, only [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]] tagged; doctor name untagged.
GOOD:
  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):
  [[রোগীর_নাম|Anjali]], [[रोगी_नाम|…]], [[PatientName|…]], [[एम.आर.एन|…]].
GOOD:
  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.
  brx/mni/sat (rare scripts) may soft-fail; do not collapse the whole run for them.

- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).

- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.

Do NOT invent types like DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER. Put calendar dates/times in plain prose.

Quality bar (BAD vs GOOD — avoid known judge/auditor failures):
Aim for PERFECT documents. Avoid these known failure modes:

BAD → dialect_script_impurity:
  English (or wrong-script) clinical body when Gujarati/Tamil/Meitei/Ol Chiki/Arabic/… is required.
GOOD:
  Full clinical prose in the assigned language AND exact script. Latin digits only inside ID tags
  (Aadhaar, phone, MRN, IFSC, email). Drug/lab names may stay Latin.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.
GOOD:
  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:
  All places grounded in persona state+district; names and ABHA coherent with PATIENT_NAME.

BAD → domain_persona_mismatch:
  Male persona with maternal delivery; age 70 with paediatric content.
GOOD:
  Sex/age in narrative and tags match persona exactly; clinical story fits that persona.

  [[HOSPITAL_NAME|…]] AND [[HOSPITAL_ID|…]]; [[DOCTOR_NAME|…]] whenever a doctor is mentioned.

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:
  AGE/PHONE/EMAIL repeated every chat turn; SMS that is a full discharge dump.
GOOD:
  Each TYPE once (twice max); SMS ≤ ~400 chars with every mandatory tag once.

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
  [[DATE|…]], [[POLICY_NUMBER|…]]; Aadhaar failing Verhoeff; toy phones like 1234567890.
GOOD:
  Only allow-listed TYPEs; dates/money in plain prose; valid-looking synthetic Aadhaar/phone.

BAD → localized / translated TYPE names (kills tag protection):

  Exact ASCII allow-list only: [[PATIENT_NAME|Anjali Mondal]], [[MRN|MRN-…]].
  Translate prose later — NEVER translate the TYPE token.

BAD → repair oscillation (fix A by breaking B):
  Round1 invents DATE to fit a table → Round2 English rewrite to clear invent →
  Round3 dialect_script_impurity → Round4 invent again.
GOOD:
  Fix ALL known issues together: correct ASCII tags + correct domain/persona +
  correct target script (or English pivot if this is the pre-translate stage).
  Never English-rewrite a finished Indic body just to repair tags.

Language SLA:
  hi/ta/te/bn/mr/… must stay fluent with tags — failures are our prompt/repair bug.

professional_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a seasoned rural household manager skilled in regional cuisine, budgeting, and minor home repairs, is channeling their practical curiosity into a modest family‑run domestic‑service training venture aimed at steady retirement income.
linguistic_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe comfortably converses in Marathi, handles Hindi with functional proficiency despite occasional slips, and uses basic English solely for simple instructions and signs, reflecting their pragmatic multilingual skill set rooted in village life.
sports_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe follows cricket passionately on the village radio, cheering for the Maharashtra Ranji team and the IPL side Royal Challengers Bangalore, and they occasionally join friendly matches at the village ground with the Kolhapur Cricket Club.
arts_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe spends evenings humming devotional bhajans and Marathi folk tunes, especially favorites by Shahir Sable and the contemporary duo Ajay‑Atul, and they also participate in village Lavni performances during festivals.
travel_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe enjoys short weekend getaways to Lonavala’s misty hills and Mahabaleshwar’s strawberry farms, occasionally dreaming of a longer visit to Goa’s beaches, yet plans trips conservatively to fit their modest budget and family duties.
culinary_persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe delights in preparing bhakri with fresh millet flour, puran poli stuffed with jaggery and lentils, and varan bhaat, often spicing them with home‑grown chilies, coriander, and a dash of hing; they favor mango‑pickle as a tangy accompaniment and relish tasting the seasonal mangoes of their orchard.
persona: Deshmukh Kalakumbe, a 51‑year‑old rural household maestro, fuses practical thrift with a curious flair for new ideas, runs a budding domestic‑service venture, chants bhajans, tracks cricket scores on the radio, and occasionally treats themselves to a shiny new radio despite a disciplined budget.
cultural_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe belongs to a Marathi-speaking farming community in the rural outskirts of Kolhapur, Maharashtra. His family name 'Deshmukh' historically denoted a village headman, and the sense of community leadership still runs in his veins. He grew up surrounded by sugarcane fields, mango orchards, and the rhythms of monsoon life. Traditional Maharashtrian customs shape his daily routine – the family gathers for a modest early‑morning meal of bhakri and zunka, festivals such as Ganesh Chaturthi, Diwali and Holi are celebrated with enthusiasm, and local folk arts like Tamasha and dhol‑tasha music are part of his cultural fabric. The rural setting has nurtured a practical mindset coupled with a curiosity about new ideas, which he balances with respect for age‑old practices. His cultural worldview reflects a blend of agrarian values, joint‑family solidarity, and a modest pride in his regional identity.
linguistic_background: Deshmukh Kalakumbe grew up speaking Marathi at home and in the village, and he has full literacy in Marathi – he can read and write the Devanagari script with ease. Hindi, taught in school, is his second language; he can converse comfortably, understands newspapers and can read simple Hindi texts, though occasional grammatical slips occur. His middle‑school education gave him basic English exposure, enough to follow simple instructions and comprehend everyday signs, but he is not fluent in speaking or writing English. Overall, his linguistic repertoire is anchored in Marathi, supplemented by functional Hindi, and a modest practical command of English for basic tasks.
skills_and_expertise: Deshmukh is adept at managing all aspects of household operations in a rural setting. His expertise includes preparing authentic Maharashtrian meals such as bhakri, puran poli, and varan bhaat; maintaining cleanliness and organizing living spaces; handling laundry with traditional washing methods; budgeting household expenses and managing cash flow; performing basic repairs like fixing leaky taps, minor carpentry, and simple electrical tasks; tending to kitchen and vegetable gardens, ensuring year‑round produce; providing caregiving support for children and elderly family members; coordinating festival preparations, including decorations, puja arrangements, and community feasts; and training junior domestic helpers in practical skills using his pragmatic yet curious approach.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Cooking authentic Maharashtrian dishes (e.g., bhakri, puran poli, varan bhaat)', 'Household cleaning and organization', 'Laundry using traditional washing methods', 'Household budgeting and cash‑flow management', 'Basic home repairs (plumbing, carpentry, simple electrical work)', 'Kitchen and vegetable garden maintenance', 'Childcare and elderly assistance', 'Festival preparation and coordination (decorations, puja, community feasts)', 'Training junior domestic helpers in practical skills', 'Problem‑solving with a pragmatic and curious approach (jugaad)']
hobbies_and_interests: Deshmukh enjoys listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs, often humming tunes while cooking. He is an avid cricket fan, following domestic tournaments and the Indian team's matches on the radio. In his spare time, he cultivates a small kitchen garden, growing tomatoes, chilies and okra, and experiments with new Maharashtrian recipes. He likes reading simple Marathi novels and short stories, especially those that reflect rural life. Community festivals attract his participation, where he helps with decorations and traditional dances. Occasionally, he attends basic computer classes at the local community centre to learn simple spreadsheet skills for household budgeting. He also likes taking leisurely walks in the fields surrounding his village, appreciating nature and reflecting on daily tasks.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to devotional bhajans and Marathi folk songs', 'Following cricket matches and tournaments', 'Cultivating a kitchen garden with vegetables', 'Experimenting with new Maharashtrian recipes', 'Reading Marathi novels and short stories about rural life', 'Participating in community festivals (decorations, traditional dances)', 'Attending basic computer classes for budgeting', 'Taking leisurely walks in village fields']
career_goals_and_ambitions: At 51, Deshmukh aims to transition from sole household work to a modest family‑run domestic‑service venture that can generate steady income for his retirement and provide employment opportunities for younger relatives. He intends to formalise his expertise by offering training workshops on practical housekeeping, cooking, and basic home‑maintenance for aspiring helpers in his village. Parallelly, he dreams of supporting his children's aspirations—ensuring his son can pursue higher education in a technical field and his daughter can start a small tailoring business. He also wishes to invest in a small piece of land for vegetable cultivation, using it both as an extra source of income and as a means to maintain his connection to the agrarian lifestyle he cherishes.

He speaks Marathi as his first language and follows a lifestyle centered on household management, budgeting, and community engagement. His routine includes listening to cricket on the radio and practicing traditional devotional bhajans.

BAD → instruction_drift:
  Assigned domain is oncology but the note narrates generic TB / unrelated complaint.

  Chief complaint, findings, and plan clearly match the assigned clinical domain.

BAD → surrogate_plausibility_collapse:
  Persona district=Faridkot but VILLAGE/HOSPITAL in another state; ABHA local-part unrelated to PATIENT_NAME.
GOOD:

BAD → entity stuffing / long SMS:

BAD → invented types / bad checksums:
